title: Elder Parāpariya: The eye goes to forms — Without restraint, without checking, Not seeing the danger, That person does not escape suffering. The ear goes…
content:
Elder Parāpariya:

The eye goes to forms —
Without restraint, without checking,
Not seeing the danger,
That person does not escape suffering.

The ear goes to sounds —
Without restraint, without checking,
Not seeing the danger,
That person does not escape suffering.

Not seeing the escape,
Enjoying odors,
That person does not escape suffering,
Because of lust for smells.

Remembering sweet and sour as supreme,
Remembering bitter as supreme,
Enslaved by the taste of craving,
The mind cannot awaken.

Remembering contacts —
Beautiful and pleasurable —
Infatuated, conditioned by lust,
One meets many sufferings.

The mind toward all these things —
All five —
If unable to guard them,
Suffering follows that person.

title: “Monks, sensual pleasures are impermanent, empty, false, of a delusive nature. Monks, this is the mere greeting of a fool built upon deception. Sensual pleasures…
content: “Monks, sensual pleasures are impermanent, empty, false, of a delusive nature. Monks, this is the mere greeting of a fool built upon deception. Sensual pleasures present and future, perceptions of sensual pleasure present and future — both belong to Māra’s realm, Māra’s domain, Māra’s bait, Māra’s food.”

title: Elder Nun Uppalavaṇṇā: “How tiresome are sensual desires! Impure, foul, full of thorns — Seeing the danger in sensual pleasures, Renunciation is firmly secure. I…
content:
Elder Nun Uppalavaṇṇā:

“How tiresome are sensual desires!
Impure, foul, full of thorns —
Seeing the danger in sensual pleasures,
Renunciation is firmly secure.
I went forth from Rājagaha,
Leaving home for homelessness.”

I know my former lives,
The divine eye is purified.
I know the minds of others,
The ear-element is purified.

I have attained psychic powers,
The destruction of defilements is attained.
I have attained the six direct knowledges —
The Buddha’s instruction is done.

With mind well subdued,
The bases of psychic power well developed,
The six direct knowledges are attained —
The Buddha’s instruction is done.

Sensual desires are like swords and spears —
They cut the aggregates to pieces.
What you call the pleasure of life —
For me, that sensual pleasure
Is no longer enticing.

Everywhere, delight is destroyed.
The mass of darkness,
The blinding darkness, has been shattered.
Know this, O Evil One —
You are Māra,
You are defeated.

title: “Monk, ‘butcher’s knife’ is a synonym for the five strands of sensual pleasure; ‘lifting the butcher’s knife’ means abandoning these five strands of sensual pleasure.…
content: “Monk, ‘butcher’s knife’ is a synonym for the five strands of sensual pleasure; ‘lifting the butcher’s knife’ means abandoning these five strands of sensual pleasure. ‘The wise man, taking the sword, dug up’ is the meaning. ‘Piece of meat’ is a synonym for delight and lust; ‘lifting the piece of meat’ means abandoning that delight and lust. ‘The wise man, taking the sword, dug up’ is the meaning. ‘Viper’ is a synonym for a monk who has destroyed the defilements; ‘let the viper be, do not touch the viper, pay homage to the viper’ is the meaning.”

title: “By developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence, all lust for sensual pleasures is abandoned, all lust for form is abandoned, all lust for existence…
content: “By developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence, all lust for sensual pleasures is abandoned, all lust for form is abandoned, all lust for existence is abandoned, all ignorance is abandoned, all conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned. Monks, just as a farmer in autumn, using a large plow, cuts through all the roots while plowing — so too, by developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence … all conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned. Monks, just as a grass-cutter cuts down grass, takes it by the top, shakes it up and down, left and right, and then throws it away — so too, by developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence … all conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned. Monks, just as when a bunch of mangoes is cut from its stalk, all the mangoes that were attached to the stalk are cut off with it — so too, by developing and cultivating the perception of impermanence … all conceit ‘I am’ is abandoned.”

title: Venerable Todeyya: “One in whom desires do not dwell, One in whom craving is not found, One who has crossed beyond doubt — What is…
content:
Venerable Todeyya:
“One in whom desires do not dwell,
One in whom craving is not found,
One who has crossed beyond doubt —
What is the liberation of such a one?”

The Blessed One replied to Todeyya:
“One in whom desires do not dwell,
One in whom craving is not found,
One who has crossed beyond doubt —
There is no other kind of liberation.”

Venerable Todeyya:
“Does such a one have longing,
Or is he free from longing?
Does he have wisdom,
Or is he still acquiring wisdom?
O all-seeing sage, explain this to me,
So that I may know what a sage is, O Sakyan.”

The Blessed One:
“He is free from longing,
Without any longing —
He is wise, not acquiring wisdom.
Thus, Todeyya, know the sage:
Possessing nothing,
Not attached to sensual existence.”

title: “Ānanda, he reflects: ‘Those low, vulgar, worldly, ignoble thoughts that are not connected with the goal and do not lead to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, peace,…
content: “Ānanda, he reflects: ‘Those low, vulgar, worldly, ignoble thoughts that are not connected with the goal and do not lead to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, peace, direct knowledge, enlightenment, Nibbāna — such as sensual thoughts, ill-will thoughts, harmfulness thoughts — I will not think those thoughts.’ In this way, he is clearly aware of that.”

title: “‘How, monks, should a monk contemplate so that his consciousness is not distracted or scattered externally, and does not abide internally, and is not agitated…
content: “‘How, monks, should a monk contemplate so that his consciousness is not distracted or scattered externally, and does not abide internally, and is not agitated by clinging? If his consciousness is not distracted or scattered externally, does not abide internally, and is not agitated by clinging, then there is no arising, origination of suffering regarding future birth, aging, and death.’ Good sirs, how is consciousness distracted and scattered externally? Here, good sirs, when a monk sees a form with the eye, consciousness pursues the sign of form, is tied to the enjoyment of the sign of form, is bound by the fetter of the enjoyment of the sign of form—this is called consciousness distracted and scattered externally. When he hears a sound with the ear … smells an odor with the nose … tastes a flavor with the tongue … feels a touch with the body … cognizes a phenomenon with the mind, consciousness pursues the sign of phenomenon, is tied to the enjoyment of the sign of phenomenon, is bound by the fetter of the enjoyment of the sign of phenomenon—this is called consciousness distracted and scattered externally. Thus, good sirs, is consciousness distracted and scattered externally.”

title: “Monks, what are the further things that should be done? ‘We must guard the sense faculties. When the eye sees a form, do not grasp…
content: “Monks, what are the further things that should be done? ‘We must guard the sense faculties. When the eye sees a form, do not grasp the general feature, do not grasp the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the eye faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise—we will restrain that cause, guard the eye, practice restraint of the eye. When the ear hears a sound … nose smells an odor … tongue tastes a flavor … body feels a touch … mind cognizes phenomena, do not grasp the general feature, do not grasp the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the mind faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise—we will restrain that cause, guard the mind, practice restraint of the mind.’”

title: “Monks, what is non-clinging and non-worry? Here, monks, a well-taught noble disciple regards form as: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is…
content: “Monks, what is non-clinging and non-worry? Here, monks, a well-taught noble disciple regards form as: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self.’ For him, that form changes and alters. When form changes and alters, no sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, or despair arise in him. He regards feeling … perception … formations … consciousness as: ‘This is not mine, this I am not, this is not my self.’ For him, that consciousness changes and alters. When consciousness changes and alters, no sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, or despair arise in him. Thus, monks, is non-clinging and non-worry.”
